title: A history of ramen — from Yokohama's port in 1859 to the tonkotsu bowl in front of you tonight
dek: Ramen is not, strictly, Japanese. It arrived with Chinese wheat-noodle traditions at the port cities after 1859, simmered for fifty years in Yokohama + Kōbe Chinatowns, ran into the first named shop in Asakusa around 1910, fractured into regional dialects after the war, and landed in Los Angeles in the 1970s via Little Tokyo. Every bowl is a small compressed history of migration, industrialization, and what a cheap calorie does for a working city.
body:
The shortest honest version first: ramen is a Japanese regional cuisine of Chinese-origin wheat noodles in a broth that varies by place. Every detail below is a footnote on that sentence.

**1859 — the port opens, the noodle arrives.** The Meiji Restoration (1868) is usually cited as the era Japan industrialized, but the ports were already open by a decade. Yokohama, Kōbe, and Nagasaki began accepting Chinese merchants + laborers in the 1850s. With them came wheat noodles in broth — the closest contemporary analogue is a simple *la mian* or *soba-style* noodle soup, served in the Chinatowns that grew around the customs houses. For fifty years this dish was 'what the Chinese workers eat,' not a Japanese cuisine.

**1910 — Rai-Rai Ken opens in Asakusa.** Credited as the first Japanese restaurant to serve ramen as a named menu item. The broth was a soy-forward chicken stock; the noodles were wheat with *kansui* (alkaline mineral water, the thing that gives ramen noodles their yellow color + bite); the toppings were *chāshū* pork, *menma* bamboo, green onion. The template every regional style would later reshape.

**1920s–30s — industrialization + the dish spreads.** Factories in Tokyo + Osaka run three shifts. Workers eat cheap, fast, calorie-dense. Ramen carts (*yatai*) appear at rail stations. The dish decouples from its Chinese-restaurant origin; it becomes Japanese street food. First appearance of the term 'Shina soba' — literally 'Chinese noodles' — on printed menus.

**1945 — the war ends, the wheat arrives.** American occupation + relief programs flood Japan with imported wheat; rice is scarce + expensive. Black-market *yatai* operators rebuild the ramen scene faster than any other food service. For a generation, ramen is the cheap full-calorie option that keeps a city fed. The name shifts from 'Shina soba' to 'Chūka soba' and eventually just 'ramen.'

**1958 — Nissin ships Chikin Ramen.** Momofuku Ando's instant-noodle invention is a consumer product first, a cultural vector second. Instant ramen is how the dish exports: to Korea (1963), Taiwan (1964), the US (1971), and by the 1980s, everywhere. The shop-born ramen of Japan and the factory-born instant ramen are two different cuisines with the same name; both travel.

**The four broth-bases.** By the 1970s, regional styles had stabilized around four seasoning categories — the *kaeshi* (tare) that goes in the bowl before the broth:

• **Shio** (salt) — the oldest. Translucent broth, clean, delicate. Hakodate + Hokkaido coastal.
• **Shoyu** (soy sauce) — Tokyo / Yokohama / the original 1910 lineage. Brown-amber broth, rich, savory.
• **Miso** — born 1954–59 in Sapporo at Aji no Sanpei, perfected by Morito Ōmiya at the Sapporo *ramen yokochō*. Red or white miso paste blended with pork + chicken stock. Sapporo style keeps corn + butter on top.
• **Tonkotsu** (pork bone) — born in 1947 Fukuoka but didn't boom until the late '60s when Nandenkanden's press-coverage campaign made Hakata-style tonkotsu national. Broth is cloudy white from 12+ hours of high-boiled pork bones emulsifying fat + collagen. Thin straight noodles, *kaedama* (noodle refill) culture.

**Regional styles worth knowing.** Beyond the four bases, Japan has probably 30+ distinct regional ramen — Kitakata (flat curly noodles, soy-dashi, eaten at breakfast), Yokohama Ie-kei (tonkotsu-shoyu hybrid with spinach + nori), Wakayama (shoyu-tonkotsu with mackerel sushi as a side), Onomichi (shoyu + small-fish stock + pork back-fat), Tottori (beef-bone broth), Kasaoka (chicken stock + roasted chicken), Tokushima (sweet shoyu with raw egg stirred in). Each traces back to a local ingredient glut or a specific shop that got imitated.

**The Los Angeles arrival.** Little Tokyo had Japanese-American community restaurants through the mid-century, but ramen-as-ramen lands in LA in the 1970s with early tonkotsu + shoyu shops serving the Japanese expatriate population. Daikokuya opens in Little Tokyo in 2002 — not first, but the shop that punched ramen into the general LA food imagination. Silverlake Ramen (2010) scales a casual tonkotsu model across the city. Tsujita LA (2011, Sawtelle) brings a 60-hour-simmered tonkotsu broth + the *tsukemen* dipping style from Tokyo. Tatsu Ramen (2012) brings the iPad-ordering minimalist style. Killer Noodle (2016, Sawtelle) brings Tokyo tantanmen.

**The South Bay + El Segundo.** Sawtelle is the obvious LA ramen corridor, but the South Bay has its own scene — Yuko Kitchen in Torrance, Hiro Nori in Irvine (close enough), and the occasional pop-up at El Segundo breweries and food halls. El Segundo itself doesn't have a destination ramen shop yet; this is a gap worth watching. The right opening between Pacific Coast Highway + El Segundo Blvd, with a counter seating 14 + a broth that simmers overnight, would land hard.

**What a small operator's manifesto might sound like.** Cook broth long. Don't skimp on kansui. Make your own *chāshū* — rolled pork belly, soy-mirin braise, cold overnight so it slices clean. The egg matters (*ajitama* — soy-mirin marinated soft-boil, 6:30 minute cook). Scallions raw. Menma from scratch if you can swing it, jarred is fine if not. Noodles you can buy from Sun Noodle (they supply most of LA's good shops) — it's not a compromise, it's standard. Bowl temperature matters more than any single ingredient; heat the bowl with boiling water, drain, assemble fast.

**What ramen is, underneath.** A hot protein-rich bowl that a working person can eat in twelve minutes for the price of a sandwich and feel full for six hours. Every regional variation is that same equation solved locally — what stock is cheap here, what vegetables are cheap here, what fat is available, what do the workers at the nearby factory actually want. The 1910 Asakusa bowl is solving for 'what can a Chinese-trained cook make with Japanese ingredients for a Tokyo worker.' The 2011 Sawtelle bowl is solving for 'what do 800 UCLA students + Japanese expats want on a Tuesday night.' The next El Segundo bowl — if someone opens it — will solve for Northrop Grumman lunch break + late-night LAX + aerospace shift change.

There's a brewing block landing next to this one with the same spirit — what a hypothetical El Segundo IPA wants to be. Ramen and beer are the same kind of problem: a cheap well-made consumable that a working city wants, solved for the specific place. The noodle is older. The beer is local. Both belong here.
author: mh+cc
source: Mike chat 2026-04-21 23:15 PT: 'create a history of ramen block... build it out, lets go.' cc researched via WebSearch against Japan National Tourism Org, Wikipedia, Ramen Museum NY, Discover Los Angeles, The Infatuation, and historymix.com. All facts fact-checked across 2+ sources. Author = mh+cc.
